label: Daniyel (Daniel) — Part 2 — Chapters 7–12
summary_plain: Daniyel chapters 7 through 12 contain the apocalyptic visions. Chapter 7: four great beasts rise from the sea — a lion, a bear, a leopard, and a terrifying fourth beast with iron teeth. The Ancient of Days takes his throne. One like a son of man comes with the clouds of heaven and is given dominion. Chapter 9: Daniyel prays and receives the vision of seventy weeks. Chapters 10-12: a final vision of wars between kings of the north and south, the time of trouble, and the resurrection — many who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads Daniyel 7-12 from eight DSS manuscripts (4QDan-a through 4QDan-e, 1QDan-a, 1QDan-b, 6QDan) and the Orit Ge'ez. The 'one like a son of man' (ke-var enash) in 7:13 is critical. In the DSS framework and in the Aramaic original, this figure is collective and national — 'the saints of the Most High' who receive the kingdom (7:18, 7:27). The Aramaic bar enash means simply 'a human being, a mortal.' The figure is like a human being — in contrast to the four beasts who represent empires. The saints of the Most High are the covenant people, not a divine individual. The NT appropriation of 'Son of Man' as a divine title for an individual (Matthew 26:64, Mark 14:62) reverses the text's own interpretation: Daniyel 7:27 explicitly states 'the kingdom and the dominion shall be given to the people of the saints of the Most High.' The kingdom is given to a people, not to a person. The DSS community at Qumran read this text as a national promise for the covenant community in the Period of Wrath.

key_terms.definition: Aramaic for 'a human being, a mortal.' Daniyel 7:13: 'One like a bar enash came with the clouds of heaven.' In the DSS framework, this is a collective figure representing the covenant people — 'the saints of the Most High' (7:18). Not a divine individual.
Daniyel 7:9: the Ancient of Days takes his throne — his garment white as snow, his hair like pure wool, his throne flames of fire. This is YHWH himself seated in judgment over the empires.

Daniyel 9:24-27: a period of seventy sevens (490 years) decreed for the covenant people. The chronological framework for the exile and restoration. Not a prediction of an individual messiah — a timetable for national redemption.

key_terms.term: Qaddishei Elyon (Saints of the Most High)
key_terms.definition: Daniyel 7:18, 7:27: 'The saints of the Most High shall receive the kingdom.' The bar enash figure of 7:13 is identified as the saints collectively — the covenant people. The kingdom is given to a people, not to a person.

fill_blank.prompt: I saw in the night visions, and behold, with the clouds of heaven there came one like a ______ — a mortal — and he came to the Ancient of Days.
fill_blank.source_quote: I saw in the night visions, and behold, with the clouds of heaven there came one like a son of man, and he came to the Ancient of Days and was presented before him.

fill_blank.prompt: But the ______ of the Most High shall receive the kingdom and possess the kingdom forever.
fill_blank.source_quote: But the saints of the Most High shall receive the kingdom and possess the kingdom forever, forever and ever.

fill_blank.prompt: And many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall ______ — some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt.
fill_blank.source_quote: And many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt.

multiple_choice.question: Who is the 'one like a son of man' in Daniyel 7?
options: A divine being incarnated as a human
A collective figure representing the saints of the Most High — the covenant people who receive the kingdom
multiple_choice.source_quote: The kingdom and the dominion and the greatness of the kingdoms under the whole heaven shall be given to the people of the saints of the Most High.

multiple_choice.question: What are the four beasts in Daniyel's vision?
options: Four successive empires — lion (Babylon), bear (Persia), leopard (Greece), and a terrifying fourth beast (Rome)
multiple_choice.source_quote: Four great beasts came up out of the sea, different from one another.

multiple_choice.question: How is the Ancient of Days described?
options: His garment white as snow, his hair like pure wool, his throne flames of fire
multiple_choice.source_quote: The Ancient of Days took his seat; his clothing was white as snow, and the hair of his head like pure wool; his throne was fiery flames.

faq.question: Why is 'Son of Man' in Daniyel 7:13 a collective national figure and not a divine individual?
faq.answer: The text interprets itself. Daniyel 7:13 describes 'one like a bar enash' (a mortal, a human being) coming with the clouds and receiving dominion. Then 7:18 identifies this figure: 'The saints of the Most High shall receive the kingdom.' Then 7:27 removes all ambiguity: 'The kingdom and the dominion and the greatness of the kingdoms under the whole heaven shall be given to the people of the saints of the Most High.' The kingdom is given to a people — not to a person. The Aramaic bar enash means simply 'a human being' — it contrasts with the four beasts, which represent inhuman empires. The human figure represents the human covenant community replacing the bestial empires. The NT appropriation of 'Son of Man' as a divine christological title (Matthew 26:64, Mark 14:62) reverses the text's own explanation. The DSS community at Qumran read Daniyel as a national promise for the covenant people during the Period of Wrath, not as a prediction of a divine incarnation. The Great Eraser operation here is precise: take a collective national image, reassign it to an individual, and use it to replace the very people it originally represented.

faq.question: What do the four beasts represent and how did the Qumran community understand them?
faq.answer: The four beasts of Daniyel 7 represent four successive empires: the lion (Babylon), the bear (Persia), the leopard (Greece/Alexander), and the terrifying fourth beast with iron teeth (Rome). The Qumran community identified Rome as the fourth beast and as the Kittim — the same empire they fought against in the War Scroll (1QM). The fourth beast 'devours the whole earth, treads it down, and breaks it to pieces' (7:23). The ten horns and the little horn represent rulers within the Roman system. The stone that destroys the image in chapter 2 and the saints who receive the kingdom in chapter 7 are the same reality: YHWH's people replacing the empires. The NT's instruction to 'render unto Caesar' (Matthew 22:21) stands in direct contradiction to Daniyel's vision — the stone does not render unto the image, it destroys it.

faq.question: What is the significance of the Ancient of Days description — white garment, wool-like hair?
faq.answer: Daniyel 7:9 describes the Ancient of Days: 'His clothing was white as snow, and the hair of his head like pure wool.' This is YHWH himself seated in judgment. The description of hair like pure wool (amar naqe) denotes texture — wool-like. This physical description of the divine figure uses African phenotypic imagery from the Afro-Asiatic cultural world the text emerges from. Revelation 1:14 repeats the description nearly verbatim: 'His head and his hair were white like wool, white as snow.' The European artistic tradition systematically depicted this figure with straight hair and European features, directly contradicting the text's own wool-hair description. The Great Eraser operates through art as powerfully as through text — every European Renaissance painting of a straight-haired divine figure is a visual contradiction of the scriptural description.
